input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin is a teleoperated 1/10‑scale autonomous vehicle that has been retrofitted with advanced sensors and control technology. Its hardware hierarchy comprises lower‑level and upper‑level chassis structures together with dedicated autonomy elements (e.g., perception and actuation modules). The vehicle operates as a miniature autonomous driving platform for Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) experiments, providing a constrained yet representative testbed that enables off‑loading of computation and remote management while preserving safety‑critical characteristics.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin delivers three core services: (1) Data Monitoring – a user interface (UI) streams real‑time vehicle state, projected driving path, and nearby object tracks, and allows users to view, save, and generate reports of the collected data; (2) Computation Offloading – the ACC algorithm (lead‑vehicle detection, speed‑set point computation) is executed on external compute resources (e.g., cloud), with the resulting desired speed sent back to the vehicle for actuation; (3) Remote Management – through the UI operators can enable or disable ACC, set a desired speed, and issue an emergency braking command. These services support low end‑to‑end latency communication, cater to two user classes (operators who monitor and control the system, and developers who troubleshoot via data collection), and are framed within a Fully Autonomous Digital Twin with a Human‑in‑the‑Loop.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The DT implementation leverages ROS 2 (Robot Operating System 2) as the middleware for real‑time data exchange and distributed node communication. The on‑board compute stack runs on NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0, providing the required drivers, CUDA libraries, and hardware abstraction for the GPU‑enabled vehicle platform. Application modules are authored in Python 3.8 for high‑level logic, C++ for performance‑critical components, and MATLAB/Simulink for model‑based design and controller synthesis. Low‑latency communication between vehicle and DT uses DDS (Data Distribution Service) bindings provided by ROS 2. Cloud or edge servers host the offloaded ACC computation, interfaced via secure, high‑bandwidth network links. The UI is built on a web‑based framework (e.g., HTML/JavaScript) that visualizes telemetry, path projections, and object tracks, and provides control widgets for remote management.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The DT requires a shared notion of time across the physical and digital sides to support computation offloading; clock synchronization must achieve 100 ms accuracy (the system limits its sync to 100 ms, whereas many safety‑critical vehicular standards demand tighter bounds). This synchronization interval defines the temporal granularity for services such as sensor data acquisition, point‑cloud clustering, and actuation commands.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The architecture follows the ISO 23247 entity‑based reference model and instantiates three functional entities – the Physical Twin Entity, the Digital Twin Entity, and the User Entity – each deployed on a separate hardware component. Each entity is realized as a single instance of its respective functional entities (e.g., the User Interface FE is split into visualization and control‑panel sub‑components). Thus the system is decentralized with a hierarchical structure of entities rather than multiple parallel twins.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Orchestration relies on the ISO 23247 reference architecture: functional entities are mapped to ROS 2 packages, each containing one or more ROS 2 nodes that implement the required functionality. The Physical Twin Entity hosts resource‑specific functions (e.g., point‑cloud clustering and tracking), the Digital Twin Entity provides data‑management and control mechanisms, and the User Entity supplies the 3D visualization and control‑panel interfaces. Coordination between entities is achieved through ROS 2 communication middleware and the 100 ms clock‑synchronisation layer, ensuring consistent execution across distributed hardware.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin comprises a geometric data component (raw LiDAR point clouds) collected from a Hokuyo UST‑10LX sensor, and a behavioral model that clusters these points using the DBSCAN algorithm and tracks the resulting clusters with a Kalman filter. An analytical component stores processed data in a relational SQL database and provides an Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) functional entity that consumes the tracked object states to compute speed commands. The model hierarchy is: raw sensor stream → clustering model → tracking model → ACC control model. Data sources include LiDAR and depth camera; data management is performed via SQL storage; model relationships are defined by sequential data flow from perception to control.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation is realized through a Vedder Electronic Speed Controller driving the vehicle’s drive motor and a steering servo mounted on the chassis. An Actuation Functional Entity converts desired speed and steering angle commands into motor voltage and servo position signals. Remote operation is provided by a Control Panel Interface that transmits commands over the network to the speed controller and steering actuator. The actuation range covers the full vehicle speed range and steering angle limits defined by the servo’s mechanical limits; safety constraints are enforced by the controller firmware.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The sensing suite consists of an onboard Hokuyo UST‑10LX 2‑D LiDAR for 2‑D range measurements and an Intel DeepSense D435i depth camera providing RGB‑D data. Additional vehicle‑level sensors capture ego speed and steering angle. Sensors are distributed on the vehicle roof (LiDAR) and front‑facing (camera). Data are sampled at the native sensor rates (LiDAR up to 10 Hz, depth camera up to 30 Hz) and transmitted via the vehicle’s Ethernet bus to the Data Collection Functional Entity, which forwards them to the perception models. Accuracy specifications follow the manufacturers’ datasheets.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensor data from the physical twin (LiDAR point‑cloud, motor status, steering servo signals, and overall vehicle state) is first processed by the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking software component, which converts raw 3D points into clustered object tracks and a projected driving path. The resulting data streams are then passed to the Data Translation functional element (FE), which serialises the messages, aligns the message types to ISO 23247 specifications and performs unit conversions for every data stream. Communication between software components inside the Digital Twin System (DTS) occurs on a ROS 2 intra‑process network, while the exchange between the Physical Twin entity and the Digital Twin entity is carried out over a Wi‑Fi link using UDP at the transport layer. The data types transmitted to the virtual side therefore include processed point‑cloud clusters, object track descriptors, vehicle state variables (e.g., speed, position, heading) and the computed driving path. Pre‑processing steps consist of point‑cloud clustering, tracking, and unit‑conversion performed by the Data Translation component before the data is stored or visualised.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The Digital Twin generates control commands for the vehicle’s Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) subsystem. Command types include enabling/disabling ACC, setting a desired speed value, and issuing an emergency braking request. These commands are formatted by the Data Translation FE (serialised, type‑aligned and unit‑converted) and transmitted to the vehicle over the same Wi‑Fi UDP link used for sensor data. Upon receipt, the Actuation functional element on the physical side decodes the messages and forwards the desired speed or brake command to the vehicle’s motor and steering actuators. The system provides execution confirmation through feedback of the updated vehicle state (e.g., actual speed), which is again processed by the Data Translation FE and sent back to the DT for monitoring. No explicit authentication or conflict‑resolution mechanisms are described in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The DT components communicate internally via the ROS 2 middleware, forming a publish/subscribe network on the hosting compute platform. Communication between the physical twin and the digital twin occurs over a wireless Wi‑Fi network using UDP as the transport protocol. The architecture thus comprises a local ROS 2 mesh for intra‑DT component interaction and an external Wi‑Fi link for inter‑entity exchange. Specific bandwidth, latency, reliability, redundancy, or security mechanisms (e.g., encryption, QoS) are not detailed in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The digital twin services are implemented as software components that can be deployed on compute resources external to the vehicle, such as cloud or edge platforms, enabling off‑loading of the ACC algorithm from the resource‑constrained ECU. Data collected from the vehicle are stored on an external device or storage service separate from the on‑board hardware. The modular service composition (Data Translation, Data Monitoring, Computation Offloading, Remote Management) allows scaling by adding more compute nodes as needed, but the documents do not specify concrete hosting platforms, scalability mechanisms, or availability guarantees.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The Digital Twin implements a data‑monitoring function that continuously captures vehicle‑state data and produces reports for operators. A computation‑offloading component hosts an Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) algorithm that, within the twin, identifies the lead vehicle and computes a desired speed, thereby providing real‑time decision support. Remote management is delivered via a user‑interface that visualises the generated reports and allows the user to issue control commands to the physical vehicle. Table I of the source lists analytics modules such as point‑cloud clustering and tracking, which process sensor streams to generate situational insights that are presented to users and can trigger automated actions.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin system complies with ISO 23247, the International Standards Organization’s Digital Twin framework for manufacturing. It adopts Part 2 (Reference Architecture), which prescribes an entity‑based architecture comprising an Observable Manufacturing Element (OME), a Data Collection and Device Control Entity, a Digital Twin Entity, and a User Entity. Each entity is further decomposed into Functional Entities that handle specific tasks such as actuation, data transformation, and user interfacing. While ISO 23247 does not dictate concrete implementation techniques, the document notes that standards‑based communication protocols such as OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA) can be employed for data exchange and control.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extracted description is highly accurate and specific, fully supported by the text. It correctly identifies the scale (1/10), origin (Traxxas teleoperated vehicle), retrofitting nature, chassis structure (lower/upper levels), and the purpose (ACC experiments). Supported by quotes: 'built off a Traxxas teleoperated vehicle', 'physical architecture consists of three layers. The lower level chassis... The upper level chassis', 'developing the DT for the ACC of a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle'.

The extraction comprehensively covers the three specific services defined in the text: Data Monitoring, Computation Offloading, and Remote Management. It details the UI capabilities, the offloading logic (ACC to external compute), user commands, and the classification of users. Supported by quotes: 'develop the following three services... 1) Data Monitoring... 2) Computation Offloading... 3) Remote Management', 'UI... display real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and detected object tracks', 'users of our system can be grouped into two classes: operators and developers'.

The extraction correctly identifies the tools and enablers: ROS 2 as middleware, NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 as the SDK, and programming languages (Python 3.8, C++, MATLAB/Simulink). The source states: 'We use NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 as the Software Development Kit (SDK) for programming the Jetson Orin Nano, and Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) as the middleware to integrate application modules. Application modules are implemented in Python 3.8... C++... and MATLAB/Simulink'. It also mentions low-latency communication via DDS (implicitly via ROS 2) and a web-based UI. The description is specific and well-supported. The score is 5.

Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately identifies the 100 ms clock synchronization accuracy and the necessity of a shared notion of time for computation offloading. It correctly contrasts the system's limit with safety-critical standards and cites the hardware limitations as the reason.

The description correctly identifies the three ISO 23247 functional entities (Physical, Digital, User) and their deployment on separate hardware components. It accurately captures the splitting of the User Interface FE and the single-instance nature of the system.

This provides a precise description of the system's orchestration, correctly mapping entities to ROS 2 packages and detailing their specific responsibilities (e.g., data management in the Digital Twin Entity, resource-specific functions in the Physical Twin Entity). It accurately identifies ROS 2 middleware as the coordination mechanism.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source text focuses on the internal architecture of the Digital Twin System (Vehicle, DT Entity, UI) and mentions 'external to the vehicle' storage, but does not describe integration patterns with external enterprise systems, other digital twins, or specific interoperability standards with outside systems.

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)

The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately reflects the source content. Table I confirms the 'Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking' component which 'Clusters raw LiDAR point clouds via DBSCAN, then tracks using a Kalman filter,' supporting the geometric and behavioral model descriptions. The 'Data Storage' component which 'Gathers object tracks and ego vehicle state into a SQL database' supports the analytical and data management claims. The model hierarchy and ACC functionality are supported by the description of the ACC component in Table I ('Determines lead vehicle and computes following speed') and the data flow described in Section IV-B.

The extraction is supported by the source but adds some specific details not explicitly present. Section III-B lists the 'Vedder Electronic Speed Controller' and implies steering control via the 'Traxxas Slash 4x4' chassis. Table I defines the 'Actuation' component which 'Converts desired speed into motor voltage commands,' matching the Actuation Functional Entity description. The 'Control Panel Interface' is listed in Table I for remote commands. However, the specific 'actuation range,' 'servo’s mechanical limits,' and 'safety constraints enforced by controller firmware' are not found in the provided text, making the score 4.

The extraction is well-supported by the text. Section III-B explicitly lists 'Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR' and 'Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera.' The 'Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking' component in Table I confirms the use of LiDAR data. The spatial distribution (roof/front-facing) and ROS2/Ethernet data transmission are consistent with the architectural description in Section III-B and Fig. 2. However, specific sampling frequencies ('10 Hz', '30 Hz') and accuracy specs following manufacturers' datasheets are not explicitly quoted in the provided snippets.

The extraction states 'Not in Document,' which is accurate as the source documents do not contain specific information regarding model fidelity requirements, validation and verification methods, uncertainty sources, or error detection mechanisms for the Digital Twin models, focusing instead on system architecture and general requirements like latency.

_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.

block_5_docs_retrieved:
In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture
